coredata.dc:title: Encapsulation of graphene quantum dot-crosslinked chitosan by carboxymethylcellulose hydrogel beads as a pH-responsive bio-nanocomposite for the oral delivery agent
coredata.dc:creator: Javanbakht, Siamak
Shaabani, Ahmad
coredata.dc:description: Oral delivery most commonly used due to the non-invasive nature and the fact that avoids patient pain and discomfort in compression with the intravenous administration. Herein, the obtained graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from citric acid were employed as a cross-linker for chitosan (CS). Sodium salicylate (SS) as a model drug was loaded in the prepared graphene quantum dots-crosslinked chitosan hybrid bio-nanocomposite beads (CS-GQD). SS-loaded CS-GQD (CS-GQD/SS) was protected with pH-sensitive biopolymeric carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) hydrogel beads. The CMC encapsulated CS-GQD/SS bio-nanocomposite hydrogel beads (CS-GQD/SS@CMC) were characterized using FT-IR, PL and SEM analysis. For determination of surficial charge of the carrier, pH point of zero charges (pHpzc) was measured. In-vitro drug delivery tests were carried out in simulating the gastrointestinal tract conditions for proving the efficiency of the prepared beads as a controlled oral drug delivery. The synergistic effects of CMC and CS enhanced the stability of drug dosing for a long time with controlling the drug releases in the gastrointestinal tract conditions. The MTT test confirmed that the bio-nanocomposite beads have low toxicity against human colon adenocarcinoma HT29 cells. The obtained results showed that the prepared novel CS-GQD/SS@CMC could potentially be used as a safe carrier for oral drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-nanocomposite
Graphene quantum dot
Oral delivery

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQDs: TEM (a), DLS (b), UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs in water with PL spectrum of the dilute aqueous solution of GQDs (c), the photograph demonstrates the fluorescence of a dilute aqueous solution of GQDs under 365 nm UV light (d) and photograph demonstrates the fluorescence of CS-GQD beads under 365 nm UV light, just after preparation (e).
FT-IR spectra for the CA, pure CS, GQDs, CS-GQD, SS, CS-GQD/SS, pure CMC, SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC.
PL spectra of the CS-GQD and CS-GQD@CMC.
Zeta potentials of the CS-GQD/SS and CS-GQD/SS@CMC.
SEM images of the CS-GQD (a), CMC@SS (c), CS-GQD/SS@CMC (e) at low magnification and SEM images of the CS-GQD (b), CMC@SS (d), CS-GQD/SS@CMC (f) at high magnifications.
Swelling behavior of CS-GQD/SS, SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC in simulated gastrointestinal tract passage (pH and time) and digital photograph of the dry and swelled CS-GQD/SS@CMC at initial and end.
Drug release behavior of CS-GQD/SS, SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC in simulated gastrointestinal tract passage (pH and time).
Results of MTT assay on HT29 cells after incubation with CS-GQD, CS-GQD/SS, SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC. Results are expressed as mean ± S.D. of three experiments.
The schematic representation of coating of CS-GQD/SS with CMC and preparation of CS-GQD/SS@CMC bio-nanocomposite hydrogel beads.

head.ce:title: Encapsulation of graphene quantum dot-crosslinked chitosan by carboxymethylcellulose hydrogel beads as a pH-responsive bio-nanocomposite for the oral delivery agent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, G. C., P. O. Box 19396-4716, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oral delivery most commonly used due to the non-invasive nature and the fact that avoids patient pain and discomfort in compression with the intravenous administration. Herein, the obtained graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from citric acid were employed as a cross-linker for chitosan (CS). Sodium salicylate (SS) as a model drug was loaded in the prepared graphene quantum dots-crosslinked chitosan hybrid bio-nanocomposite beads (CS-GQD). SS-loaded CS-GQD (CS-GQD/SS) was protected with pH-sensitive biopolymeric carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) hydrogel beads. The CMC encapsulated CS-GQD/SS bio-nanocomposite hydrogel beads (CS-GQD/SS@CMC) were characterized using FT-IR, PL and SEM analysis. For determination of surficial charge of the carrier, pH point of zero charges (pHpzc) was measured. In-vitro drug delivery tests were carried out in simulating the gastrointestinal tract conditions for proving the efficiency of the prepared beads as a controlled oral drug delivery. The synergistic effects of CMC and CS enhanced the stability of drug dosing for a long time with controlling the drug releases in the gastrointestinal tract conditions. The MTT test confirmed that the bio-nanocomposite beads have low toxicity against human colon adenocarcinoma HT29 cells. The obtained results showed that the prepared novel CS-GQD/SS@CMC could potentially be used as a safe carrier for oral drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, controlled drug release is great importance. Hence, it has attracted more consideration in medicine due to their advantages, for instance, safety, sustained release time, biocompatibility, biodegradability and reduced the drug poisoning effect via decreasing the drug release rate thereby [1,2]. In enhancing drug efficiency, reducing side effects and safe drug administration, drug delivery system (DDS) acts as a critical role [3,4]. By employment DDS, the problems of traditional administration are resolved via solubilization of drug, reducing side effects and maintaining the biodegradability and bioactivity [5]. Controlling of drug release and organizing of the drug dose at a certain required level for a lengthening duration could prevent the side effects of burst drug release.

Among the most commonly occurring diseases, intestinal disease is the fourth [6]. In this case, site-specific oral delivery of drug could potentially to enhance the drug efficiency [7]. Since for the treatment of inflammation and for curing diseases including wound healing [8], diabetes [9], arthritis [10] and cancer treatment [11], sodium salicylate (SS) is widely used as prescribed agents it can well chose as the model drug. Due to erratic absorption of SS through the gastrointestinal tract conditions (GIT) [12], the oral conventional delivery of it need to manage. Currently, among various carriers as DDS including dendrimers [13], microspheres [14], synthetic and natural polymeric particles [15,16], liposomes [17] and nanomaterials [18], bio-nanocomposites have demonstrated more attention as drug carriers [19].

GQDs have recently received an increasing attention, due to their strong quantum confinement and edge effects [20,21]. Synthesis of GQDs (bottom-up and top-down methods) and determination of their properties are dedicated enormous efforts [22]. GQDs have excellent properties, for example, large surface area with delocalized electrons, high fundamental fluorescence, unparalleled excitation relevant emission, chemical inertness, solubility in the variety of solvents, local functional groups at the edges, capability of drug loading by π-π interactions and easy variability of shape and size. For biomedical applications, GQDs performed as talented materials [23,24]. Besides, for cellular imaging and drug delivery GQDs have been extensively investigated [25,26]. Because of GQDs structure are totally composed of “carbon” atoms they are nontoxic in comparison with inorganic quantum dots that possibly have toxic metal ions after degradation in their residues [27–29].

For the aim of DDS, biopolymers as raw materials because of their unique properties, such as non-toxicity, biodegradability, biocompatibility and environmental sensitivity, etc. have been broadly used [30,31]. In wide variety of biomedical application, bio-polymeric hydrogels owing to their unique characteristic have been gained tremendous importance [32,33]. Hydrogels are 3D cross-linked linear or branched polymers with hydrophilic features. In comparison with common absorbent materials, hydrogels have the capability to absorb large amounts of water, physiological solutions and saline [34]. The generally used biopolymers for preparing hydrogels are polysaccharide, for instance, CS, alginate, agar, carrageenan, pectin, cellulose and derivatives of it [35–38].

CS is one of the most abundant natural biopolymers, which has concerned many attentions in the medical and pharmaceutical applications [39,40]. CS has a potential to be used as a suitable carrier in DDS due to its film- and gel-forming properties [41]. Furthermore, CS can form spherical gel beads in the presence of polyanionic molecules. Owing to the mildness and simplicity of this method and creation of small and uniform size shape in comparison with the conventional hydrogels, CS in the form of gel beads has gained more attraction as a drug controlled release protocol [42]. In the present work, for the first time, we have applied anionic GQDs as a CS crosslinker to avoid cytotoxicity of the conventional cross-linkers (glutaraldehyde, epichlorohydrin and other bifunctional relatives) [43] and prepared CS-GQD hybrid beads then SS was loaded in the CS-GQD (Scheme 1 ). In acidic pH, CS is soluble because of the amino groups protonation [44]. Therefore, in the gastric acidic environment, drug release from the CS-based delivery agents is limited and expected in the small intestine. However, for oral drug delivery, the drug release should be accelerated and expected in the all of intestine. An effective solution for this problem improved by encapsulation of CS-based delivery agent into biocompatible bio-polymeric supports to bypass the gastric pH [45,46].

CMC is a cellulose carboxymethyl derivation, which has exclusive features such as non-toxicity, pH-sensitivity, hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, and gel-forming properties [47]. Application of CMC-based hydrogels in numerous fields makes CMC as a great candidate for biomedical applications [16,19,48]. CMC produces sphere-shaped gel beads in the presence of ionic crosslinkers, for instance, Al3+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ [16]. This method to be able to attracted great attention in the field of drugs controlled release owing to the characteristics including mildness, simplicity, and uniform beads formation [49]. Since at low pH CMC is stable and resistant against proteases and amylases, so CMC-based hydrogels would preserve any of drug or drug-carrier agent in the upper GIT conditions. In the point of this view, for intestinal drug delivery, the CMC is chosen as a suitable properties polymer for coating agent which can provide the required specific delivery [50,51].

The aim of present work to prepare a suitable oral carrier via selectively coating of drug-carrier agent with the biopolymeric hydrogel. We present the development of CMC for site-specific oral drug delivery using a more efficient methodology and a novel combination of CS-GQD (Scheme 1). This biocompatible polysaccharide with shrinking properties in acidic conditions could protect the drug-loaded CS-GQD against stomachic degradation, which is suitable for a potential specific release of the drug in colon. The performance of the prepared system in the controlled delivery of SS was investigated in the simulated GIT conditions.

CS (molecular weight: 100,000–300,000) was purchased from Acros Organics. CMC with a degree of substitution about 0.55–1.0 and viscosity of 15,000 mPas/s (1% in H2O, 25 °C) purchased from Nippon Paper Industries (Japan). Citric acid (CA), sodium salicylate (SS) and iron (III) chloride (FeCl3·6H2O) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Co. All solvents used for the experiments were of analytical grade and used without further purification and all other materials purchased from Merck Co.

UV–Vis absorption spectra obtained by Analytic Jena (Specord 210) UV–vis spectrophotometer, German. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were made in KBr pellets on a Shimadzu IR470 spectrometer. Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) was conducted by LEO 906E transmission electron microscope operating. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (LEO 1430VP instrument) used to determine the surface morphology of the samples. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were received by a spectrofluorometric (LS45, PerkinElmer).

In a typical procedure, according to the previous reports via direct pyrolysis of CA [21], 2 g CA was put into a 5 mL beaker and heated up to 200 °C using a heating mantle. About 30 min later, the obtained orange liquid was added dropwise into 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After neutralizing, the 1% stock solution of GQDs were prepared and kept in the dark to further use.

CS-GQD bio-nanocomposite hydride beads were prepared according to a previously reported by some modification [52], using GQDs as cross-linking agent. First, 0.1 g of CS was dissolved in 10 mL 0.1 M acetic acid solutions. Then, the solution was extruded in the form of droplets, into 10 mL of 1% GQDs stock solution, using a syringe (2 mm diameter). The beads were stayed in the solution for 24 h in order to crosslink with GQDs. After that, the beads were filtered and for several times washed with distilled water to remove un-reacted GQDs. Finally, the beads were dried under vacuum for 24 h.

Typically, 100 mg of CS-GQD was immersed in drug solution (100 mg SS, in 10 mL water) and shacked 150 rpm at room temperature for 72 h. The solvent was removed by filtration and washed rapidly with distilled water to remove unloaded drug. CS-GQD/SS was dried in the vacuum oven for 24. The amount of loaded drug in the CS-GQD was measured by UV–Vis spectroscopy. To estimate the drug content in the CS-GQD, the free amount of SS was collected from the solution and then the amount of drug determined by using the calibration curve of the standard SS at λ = 297 nm with UV–Vis spectroscopy. The Eqs. (1) and (2) were respectively used to determine the loading efficiency (LE) and loading capacity (LC).
(1)
% LE =
Mass of drug in nanocarrier
Mass of drug
fed
initially
∗ 100
(2)
% L C =
Mass of drug in nanocarrier Mass of nanocarrier
∗ 100

For the preparation of SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC, 0.05 g of CS-GQD/SS and 0.01 g of SS were dispersed into 25 mL distilled water, respectively. In the second step, 25 mL of a solution containing 0.5 g CMC polymer gradually and individually was added to the first solution. The obtained mixture was stirred at room temperature on the stirrer to ensure the complete dispersion of SS and CS-GQD/SS between the polymer chains. Then, the solution was extruded in the form of droplets, using a syringe (2 mm diameter), into 100 mL of ferric chloride hexahydrate (0.01 M). The beads were stayed in the solution for 20–30 min in order to crosslink with Fe3+. After that, the beads were filtered and for several times washed with distilled water to remove un-reacted Fe3+. Finally, the beads were dried under vacuum for 24 h.

The pHpzc is typically achieved by determination of the pH at which the net surface charge on soil particles was zero [53]. At this pH, the difference between the initial and the final pH (∆pH) is zero. Briefly, for measurement of pHpzc, samples were mixed mechanically in various acid and alkaline pH. Then pHpzc as determined by variance the initial pH and final pH from the following Eq. (3):
(3)
∆ pH =
pH final
−
pH initial

The in-vitro swelling tests carried out in a simulated GIT condition as previously reported with some modifications [54,55]. For this purpose, the prepared beads were consecutively immersed in 10 mL of buffered solutions with pH 1.2 (for 2 h), 6.8 (for 2 h), and 7.4 (for 4 h) to determine the swelling percentage. The swelled hydrogel bio-nanocomposite beads were taken out, blotted with filter paper to remove excess water, and weighed at the certain time intervals. The swelling percentage calculated with the Eq. (4):
(4)
Swelli n g
Rate =
Ws − Wd
Wd
where, W
d and W
s are the weights of the dry and swollen samples, respectively.

The in-vitro drug release tests carried out in a simulated GIT conditions as the previously reported with some modifications [54,55]. To determine in-vitro drug release, 0.01 g of prepared samples were consecutively immersed in 10 mL of buffered solutions with pH 1.2 (for 2 h), 6.8 (for 2 h), and 7.4 (for 4 h). At certain time intervals, adequate amounts of samples picked up and same amount of fresh buffer was replaced in order to maintain the volume of buffer in a constant value. The amount of releasing drug was measured using UV–Vis spectrophotometer at λ = 296 nm. The percent of released drug was determined using the following Eq. 5:
(5)
Drug release =
the amount of released drug the amount of loaded drug
× 100

To evaluation the in-vitro cytotoxicity of the prepared bio-nanocomposite beads, the human colon adenocarcinoma HT29 cell line was selected. MTT assay was used to evaluate the relative cell viability. In summary, the human HT29 cell lines were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 5000 cells/well in 100 μL cell medium and incubated overnight. Then cell monolayers were washed with 1× PBS. The number of cells were manually determined using a haemacytometer. Afterward, cells were treated with CS-GQD, CS-GQD/SS, SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC bio-nanocomposites. Subsequently, 20 μL of 5 mg/mL of MTT (3‑(4,5‑dimethylthiazol‑2‑yl)‑2,5‑diphenyltetrazolium bromide) in PBS was added to each well and incubated for an additional 4 h at 37 °C. The culture media were taken out and then 1 mL of HCl in isopropanol (0.04 M) was added to solubilize the formazan crystals. In 200 μL of DMSO, the purple-blue MTT formazan precipitate was dissolved. On a multi-well plate reader (Quant Bio-tek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA) the absorbance values at 570 nm were determined. Culturing experiments were performed in triplicate.

Scheme 1 illustrates an offered schematic for synthesis of CS-GQD through the crosslinking of CS by GQD nanoparticles, which roughly corresponds to the expected experimental. Then, preparation of CS-GQD/SS@CMC bio-nanocomposite hydrogel beads via coating of CS-GQD/SS hybrid beads with CMC biopolymer were proposed. In the stock solution of GQD nanoparticles, the cationic groups of CS (NH3 +) interact with anionic groups of GQDs (CO2 −) via electrostatic interactions and produce a network polymer [52]. By dropping a solution of CS in the GQDs solution, due to the physical cross-linking, the sphere materials were formed and created CS-GQD hybrid bio-nanocomposite beads. Also, the CMC encapsulated CS-GQD/SS was obtained by dropping a mixture of CMC and CS-GQD/SS aqueous dispersion solution into the ferric chloride solution. In aqueous solutions of ferric chloride, due to the presence of carboxylate groups (CO2 −) in the CMC structure, CMC easily complexes to the Fe3+ cations and cationic groups of CS (NH3 +) to produce a network polymer [56]. Also, CMC complexes to the anionic groups of GQDs (CO2 −) via Fe3+ cations and generate a network polymer [57,58]. So, owing to the physical cross-linking, sphere materials were formed when the CS-GQD/SS, CMC and ferric chloride solution reaches together. For the coating of various agents, this method could be simple and economically not requiring heat or any other tools.

By measuring at least 100 monodispersed GQDs under TEM the average diameter of 12.6 ± 1.1 nm was calculated (Fig. 1a). The hydrodynamic diameters measured using DLS experiment. As shown in Fig. 1b, the hydrodynamic diameters of GQDs is 15.3 ± 1.9 nm. A narrow full-width sharp absorption band at 362 nm (Fig. 1c) established that the sp2 clusters in GQDs should be uniform in size. The maximum emission wavelength of GQDs was found at 460 nm (Fig. 1c). These characterizing of GQDs that is shown in Fig. 1 individually is a good evidence for the synthesis of GQDs from CA [59]. As well as, photoluminescent properties were created in the bio-nanocomposite beads by crosslinking of CS with GQDs (Fig. 1e).

The FT-IR spectra are considered to understand the interactions among the materials. As seen in Fig. 2 , the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs show the skeletal vibration of aromatic rings around 1450–1650 cm−1 and stretching vibration of CH in aromatic rings around 3000–3100 cm−1. As well as, the absorption of CH bending vibration in the range lower 1350 cm−1 and stretching vibration of CH at 2950 cm−1, proposing the GQD contain some incompletely carbonized CA. As shown in CS spectrum, the CO stretching vibrations of NHCO and the NH bending vibrations of NH2 appeared at 1604 and 1590 cm−1, respectively. Compared with pure CS and GQDs, the peaks around 1600–1750 cm−1 have become broader in the spectra of GQDs-CS that can be attributed to the H-bonding associations between GQDs and CS. In the CMC FT-IR spectrum, the stretching vibration of CH2 is located at 2930 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl groups were observed around the 3400 cm−1. SS FT-IR spectrum presents the CH band in aromatic rings at 3124 cm−1 and the CO band at 1595 cm−1. The FT-IR spectrum of CS-GQD/SS@CMC is a combination of CS-GQD, SS and CMC FT-IR spectra that have characteristic bands related to the CS-GQD, SS, and CMC. Similarly, in SS@CMC FT-IR spectrum can be seen characteristic bands relevant to the SS, and CMC.

The PL spectra of CS-GQD and CS-GQD@CMC are good evidence for presence of GQDs in the bio-nanocomposite (Fig. 3 ). The PL spectrum of CS-GQD and CS-GQD@CMC in compare to GQDs spectrum in water (Fig. 1b) become wider and a splitting pattern in the spectrum was observed, which mainly due to creation of phenomenon's including intersystem transitions, external and internal transformations and self-absorbing [21]. By CMC encapsulating of CS-GQD, the various factors, for instance, inner photoluminescence reabsorption, filter effect and partial quenching [21] led to a decrease in PL intensity of CS-GQD@CMC, which is detectable in the PL spectrum of CS-GQD@CMC. Fluorescent properties were indicated that the prepared bio-nanocomposite beads could potentially be used as a fluorescent bioimaging.

For any drug carrier, the pHpzc and surface functional groups are important characteristics that indicate the net surface charge of the carrier in solution. Soil particles surface charge is generally appointment through the pH of the adsorbate solution. It is well known the net charge on soil particles surface is negative when the pH of adsorbate solution is higher than that corresponding to the pHpzc and is positive when the pH of adsorbate solution is lower than pHpzc [60]. Therefore, for investigation of CS-GQD/SS@CMC as an oral delivery, determination of pHpzc is very important. Results demonstrated that the pHpzc of CS-GQD/SS and CS-GQD/SS@CMC are 5.4 and 2.1, respectively (Fig. 4 ). Because SS is negatively charged in the range of various pH [61], thus, the positively charged CS-GQD/SS at pH < pHpzc should be interacted beneficially with the negatively charged drug and prevent the drug release in gastric fluid (pH 1.2). But, in acidic pH, CS is soluble because of the amino groups protonation [44]. Therefore, in the gastric acidic environment, SS was dramatically released. While CMC hydrogel is not soluble in the gastric acidic environment due to the shrinkage characteristics of it at acidic pH [62]. As well as, the negatively charged CS-GQD/SS@CMC at pH > pHpzc protected beneficially the positively charged CS-GQD/SS and prevent the solution of CS in acidic zone of gastric fluid [50,63].

Drug carrier morphology is one of the main factors that affecting the release manners of the drug. The surface morphology of the CS-GQD exhibited many cavities and severe wrinkles, which was probably caused by crosslinking of CS with GQDs (Fig. 5a and b). As seen in Fig. 5c and e, there are only a little difference between the morphology of CS-GQD/SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS. As a result, can be concluded the CS-GQD/SS was completely encapsulated by CMC. In addition, the high magnification SEM images demonstrate that the surface of the CS-GQD, CMC@SS and CS-GQD/SS@CMC are highly wrinkled with many cavities in the internal zones. This structure could result in easier diffusion of water and PO4 3− into the beads and faster SS release from the CS-GQD/SS, SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC.

The swelling percentage is one of the most important factors affecting the application of the hydrogels. Several parameters for example pH, composition, ionic strength, temperature, and extent of crosslinking in the network, affected the swelling amount. The swelling behavior of CS-GQD/SS was illustrated the weight loss in the simulated first zone of intestinal fluid (pH 1.2) that is related to the solution of CS [44]. Most of the carboxylate anions in CMC are protonated at pH 1.2, so under these conditions, among carboxylic acid groups, the hydrogen-bonding interactions is generated. Owing to the formation of H-bonding that is one of the physical crosslinker, the network tends to shrink and as a result, the swelling values are least (Fig. 6 ). By increasing the pH up to 7.4, the swelling degree was increased compared to acidic medium, which contributed to the electrostatic repulsion between carboxylate anions by deprotonating of the carboxylic groups in the CMC network. Generally, CMC show the pH-dependent swelling property and the optimum pH to achieve a maximum swelling ratio of CMC hydrogel is about 6–8. A little change in the CMC matrix in this range leads to a significant change in swelling [62]. Indeed, at pH 6.8 (acidic pH) the penetration of acid fluids into the CS-GQD/SS@CMC network increased due to CS solution, so as a changing phenomenon in the CMC matrix swelling ratio was dramatically increased compared to SS@CMC.

In-vitro drug release experiments carried out in simulated conditions with the in-vivo process to elucidate the potential of the prepared beads as an oral drug delivery system. For this purpose, during several specific period, in the medium with pH 1.2, 6.8, and 7.4 the beads were consecutively suspended. Fig. 7 illustrates the drug release profiles of the CS-GQD/SS, SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC in 8 h at 37 °C. Comparing the diagrams, the drug release in the first zone of intestinal fluid (pH 1.2) has the lowest drug release rate for SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC. It is because of the shrinkage of CMC at acidic pH. For CS-GQD/SS, due to the solution of CS, can be seen a fastest drug release rate at pH 1.2. After that, the release rate dramatically increased for CS-GQD/SS and SS@CMC, which might be related to the more solution of CS and pH-sensitive nature of CMC polymer, respectively. In the second zone of intestinal fluid (pH 6.8), by deprotonation of CMC carboxylate groups and repulsing between the negatively charged SS drug and CMC, can be detected a radically release for SS@CMC. Moreover, the release percentage for CS-GQD/SS@CMC is sustainable in comparison with others. In the gastrointestinal tract conditions due to the CMC and CS synergistic effects, CMC cause to decrease the drug release in the gastric fluid (pH 1.2) and CS lead to more controlling of drug release in the intestinal fluid (pH 6.8 and 7.4). These synergistic effects is related to the materials protection capability of CMC in acidic media [16,51] and interaction of CS with the negatively charged SS drug in alkaline media [52].

Cell viability experiments were carried out using the human colon adenocarcinoma HT29 cell lines and results are presented in Fig. 8 . All the prepared bio-nanocomposites demonstrated low cytotoxicity and the cell viability remained over 75%, similar results were reported by previous works [16]. The cell viability values of the CS-GQD, CS-GQD/SS, SS@CMC and CS-GQD/SS@CMC were 80.6, 71.6, 70.3 and 70.8, respectively. This proves the cytocompatibility of the prepared bio-nanocomposites as oral delivery system.

Summery, in the first step, GQDs were prepared from CA and characterized. Preparation of GQDs was proved by various analysis including TEM, DLS, FT-IR, UV–Vis, and PL spectrum. In the second step, CS was crosslinked with GQDs, then, CS-GQD/SS was prepared by the immersing of CS-GQD hybrid bio-nanocomposite beads in the SS drug solution. In the third step, CS-GQD/SS was encapsulated with CMC hydrogel beads. Furthermore, the FT-IR and PL analysis of CS-GQD/SS@CMC confirmed the existence of CS-GQD within the CMC biopolymer matrix. SS release studies showed that CMC provides a better protection against the stomach acidic environment. Controlled release in the GIT conditions and stability of drug dosing for a long period for CS-GQD/SS@CMC was observed, which is related to the synergistic effects of CMC and CS. Cytotoxicity results demonstrated that the prepared bio-nanocomposites have a low toxicity against HT29 cells. The obtained results showed that the CS-GQD/SS@CMC could be a well propose as a potential oral drug delivery.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the Research Council of Shahid Beheshti University.
